title: Where Grammar Lives: Syntactic Transition Priors in MLP Vectors
subtitle: We matched MLP input/output vectors across tokens in GPT-2 and Pythia and found something unexpected: the displacement vectors encode syntactic transition probabilities — replicated across four models with statistical rigor.
author: Peter Balogh

children.children: Every transformer MLP takes in a vector and spits out a different vector. The difference between them — the displacement — is the MLP's 'opinion' about that token. But what IS that opinion?

Most interpretability work focuses on what MLPs amplify or suppress in the residual stream. We asked a different question: if you take one token's MLP output and find the most similar MLP input across ALL tokens, what do you get? Is there sequential structure hiding in these vectors?

The answer floored us. The displacement vectors don't encode semantics. They don't encode token identity. They encode grammar — specifically, syntactic transition probabilities. The MLP is whispering: 'after a preposition, expect a determiner.'

Think of each MLP as a worker on an assembly line. The worker receives a partially-built widget (the input vector), does some work on it, and passes it forward (the output vector). The displacement is the work itself — the twist of the wrench, the dab of glue. We're studying the work, not the widget. And it turns out the work encodes what the NEXT worker should expect to receive.

We started with 90 diverse English sentences through GPT-2 Small, then scaled to 1,000 sentences from the Brown corpus across four models (GPT-2 Small, Medium, Large, and Pythia 410M) — over 16,000 tokens with proper NLTK POS tagging. For each token at multiple layers, we grabbed:

• The MLP pre-activation vector (input) — what the residual stream hands to the MLP
• The MLP post-activation vector (output) — what the MLP hands back
• The displacement = output − input — the MLP's contribution

Why three layers? Layer 0 is the first MLP the token embedding ever encounters. Layer 5 is mid-network. Layer 11 is the final MLP before the unembedding. If there's grammatical structure in these displacements, we want to see how it evolves.
Each vector lives in GPT-2's 768-dimensional residual stream space. The displacement is also 768-dimensional — it's the direction and magnitude of the MLP's push on the residual stream.

Every token was also tagged with its part of speech (POS): DET (the, a), PREP (on, in, of), NOUN, VERB, ADJ, AUX (is, was, has), PRON (he, she, it), CONJ (and, but), and a catch-all CONTENT category for open-class words. This POS labeling is what let us see the grammar.
Our first attempt was naive. We computed cosine similarities between all displacement vectors and got... mush. Everything looked similar to everything else. Cosine similarities were uniformly high.

The culprit: a dominant mean direction. When you average all 1,133 displacement vectors, you get a single strong direction that accounts for most of the variance. This is the 'average MLP effect' — the generic push that every token gets, regardless of identity. It swamps the interesting stuff.

Imagine you're studying how different birds fly. But there's a 50 mph wind blowing east. Every bird is being pushed east. If you measure their trajectories, they all look similar — eastward. The fix is obvious: subtract the wind. Now you can see the eagle soaring, the hummingbird hovering, the swallow diving. The mean displacement is the wind. Subtract it, and the individual flight patterns emerge.

The fix: compute the mean displacement vector across all tokens, then subtract it from every individual displacement. Now each vector represents only the token-specific deviation from the average MLP behavior.

This mean-subtraction step is critical. Without it, PCA finds nothing interesting, cosine matching is meaningless, and the entire analysis collapses into a single dominant direction. If you're doing displacement analysis on ANY transformer component, subtract the mean first.
With the mean subtracted, we applied PCA to the displacement vectors at Layer 0. The results were immediate and striking.

PC1 captures 25–36% of the variance — a massive amount for a 768-dimensional space. And what does it separate? Function words from content words. Determiners, prepositions, conjunctions, and auxiliaries cluster on one end. Nouns, verbs, and adjectives cluster on the other. With proper NLTK POS tagging on 16,000+ tokens, the separation gets even cleaner: NOUN (-17.6), ADJ (-14.6), VERB (-14.2) vs CONJ (+25.7), DET (+22.1), PREP (+19.7).
The single most important axis of variation in MLP displacements is the function/content word distinction. A quarter of everything the MLP does, token by token, is navigating this boundary.

PC2 (9.9% variance) separates sentence-initial tokens from mid-sentence tokens. The MLP knows where you are in the sentence — not because position is encoded in the displacement directly, but because the grammatical expectations differ at position 1 versus position 15.

PC3 (5.6% variance) is subtler: it distinguishes narrative/animate content (people, actions, stories) from abstract content (concepts, quantities, relations). Even within content words, the MLP carves grammatically-relevant distinctions.

props.title: What 'Function vs. Content' Means for MLPs

children.children: In linguistics, function words (the, is, of, and) are the scaffolding of sentences — they carry grammatical information but little semantic content. Content words (cat, run, beautiful) carry meaning. This is a centuries-old distinction.

But why would an MLP care? Here's the key insight: function words and content words have radically different TRANSITION statistics. After 'the', you almost always get a noun or adjective. After 'ran', you might get a preposition, an adverb, a period — much more varied. The MLP's displacement along PC1 isn't encoding a linguistic theory. It's encoding a prediction about what comes next.

Function words are high-constraint contexts (few likely successors). Content words are low-constraint contexts (many possible successors). PC1 is a constraint axis — how tightly does this token constrain the next one?
Not all layers are equally grammatical. We measured the 'discriminative fraction' — how much of the displacement variance is explained by POS category — at each layer.
The results are dramatic and consistent across all four models:

• Pythia 410M Layer 0: 70.7% discriminative — nearly three-quarters of what the MLP does is POS-related!
• GPT-2 Medium Layer 0: 43.9% → drops to 10.4% by Layer 23
• GPT-2 Large Layer 0: 31.7% → drops to 14.6% by Layer 35
• GPT-2 Small Layer 0: 26.5% → 25.3% by Layer 11 (too shallow to see the full decay)
Bootstrap 95% confidence intervals are tight (±2-3%), confirming these aren't noise. Pythia Layer 0: 0.709 [0.685, 0.734].

The MLP's grammatical work is front-loaded. Layer 0 does the heavy lifting of establishing syntactic category, and later layers progressively shift to other concerns. This is consistent with the 'syntax early, semantics late' pattern found in probing studies.

Layer 0 is the foundation and framing — you establish the structural category of each element (is this a load-bearing wall or a window?). Layer 5 is the electrical and plumbing — functional systems that depend on structure but go beyond it. Layer 11 is the paint and furniture — the final details that make the house specific. You don't pour foundations on the last day.

Here's where it gets wild. We took each token's MLP output vector and asked: which token's MLP input vector is most similar to this output? We're essentially following the arrows — if the MLP pushes 'on' to a certain place, whose input lives near that place?

We used a combined similarity metric: 70% endpoint cosine similarity (comparing the output of token A to the input of token B) plus 30% direction cosine similarity (comparing displacement directions). This balances 'where does it point?' with 'how does it move?'
The results:
This is where model size matters enormously. Next-token accuracy from displacement cosine alone:

• GPT-2 Small: 2.7% at Layer 11 (31× chance)
• GPT-2 Medium: 5.5% at Layer 23 (63× chance)
• GPT-2 Large: 21.2% at Layer 17 (243× chance!)
• Pythia 410M: 11.1% at Layer 18 (127× chance)

Read that GPT-2 Large number again: one in five tokens can be predicted just by matching MLP displacement geometry. No language model, no attention, no residual stream — just 'where did the MLP push this token?' And crucially, the peak is at Layer 17 out of 36 — about 50% depth. Not the final layer. The syntactic transition prior is an intermediate computation.
Same-POS accuracy ranges from 70-95% across models. The MLP output is landing in the right grammatical neighborhood even when it doesn't nail the exact token.

But the most revealing statistic: 41% of all matches are PREP→DET. Prepositions like 'on', 'in', 'of' get matched to determiners like 'the', 'a'. This isn't random — it's the single most common syntactic bigram in English. The MLP's geometry has internalized the grammar of English prepositional phrases.

Token: 'on' (PREP, sentence: 'The cat sat on the mat')
MLP output at Layer 5 → cosine match → MLP input of 'the' (DET, from a DIFFERENT sentence)

The MLP pushes 'on' toward a region of vector space where determiners live as inputs. It's not matching the specific word 'the' — it's matching the grammatical category DET. The MLP has encoded: after a preposition, expect a determiner.

To go beyond individual matches, we built POS-to-POS transition matrices from the displacement similarities. For every pair of POS categories (X, Y), we computed: how similar are X's MLP outputs to Y's MLP inputs, on average? Positive means 'X's output points toward Y's input region.' Negative means 'X's output points AWAY from Y's input region.'
The transition matrix tells us which grammatical transitions the MLP encodes as natural (positive) and which it encodes as unlikely (negative).
Here are the key findings across layers:

PREP→DET: Universally significant (p<0.001) in EVERY model tested. In GPT-2 Large it reaches +0.60 at Layer 0, and in Pythia it goes from -0.03 (not yet learned!) at Layer 0 to +0.60 at Layer 12. Permutation tests (500 shuffles) confirm: the real signal is 5-10× the null distribution. In the scaled 16K-token experiment, 39 out of 83 POS transitions are significant at p<0.01.

DET→CONTENT: Anti-correlated at -0.08 by Layer 11. This is surprising — determiners DO precede content words (nouns, adjectives). But the MLP suppresses this transition in displacement space. Why? Possibly because the specific content word after 'the' is highly variable and context-dependent, so the MLP doesn't try to predict it geometrically.

Other strong positive transitions:
• DET→DET (+0.35 at L5): 'the' → 'the' patterns (e.g., across clause boundaries)
• AUX→AUX (+0.31 at L5): auxiliary chains like 'has been'
• PRON→AUX (+0.28 at L5): 'he is', 'she was', 'they have'
• CONJ→DET (+0.42 at L5): 'and the', 'but a'
• PREP→DET (+0.58 at L5): the dominant pattern

Notice the pattern: the strongest transitions are all function→function word patterns. The MLP geometry primarily encodes the transitions BETWEEN grammatical scaffolding words. Content words are more variable and harder to predict categorically.

Think of the transition matrix as a heat map. Rows are 'source POS' (whose MLP output are we looking at?) and columns are 'target POS' (whose MLP input are we comparing to?). Bright cells mean the MLP output of the source POS points toward the input region of the target POS.

At Layer 0, the matrix is warm but diffuse — many moderate positive values. By Layer 5, it sharpens dramatically: PREP→DET becomes a hot spot, function→function transitions strengthen, and function→content transitions weaken or go negative. By Layer 11, the matrix is sparse — only the strongest transitions survive, and some cells have flipped sign entirely.
This progressive sharpening tells us the network refines its syntactic priors layer by layer. It starts with broad grammatical categories and converges on specific transition patterns.
Something genuinely strange happens between early and late layers — but only in larger models. We correlated PC1 projections from the first layer to the last:

• GPT-2 Small (12 layers): r = +0.98 — NO flip. Too shallow.
• GPT-2 Medium (24 layers): r = -0.18 — FLIPPED! Function/content polarity reverses.
• GPT-2 Large (36 layers): r = +0.47 — partial, still positive but weakened.
• Pythia 410M (24 layers): r = +0.36 — partial.

The polarity flip is model-size dependent. GPT-2 Small doesn't have enough layers. GPT-2 Medium, with 24 layers, is the sweet spot where the flip is cleanest. This suggests the flip requires sufficient depth to develop — it's a property of deep networks, not shallow ones.

props.title: Why Would the Network Flip Polarity?

children.children: One hypothesis: early layers need to identify what grammatical category a token belongs to (input classification), while later layers need to prepare for what comes next (output prediction). These are complementary tasks, and complementary tasks often have inverted geometric signatures.

At Layer 0, a function word gets a negative displacement because the MLP is saying 'this IS a function word — move it toward the function word region.' At Layer 11, a function word gets a positive displacement because the MLP is saying 'the thing AFTER a function word should be a content word — push toward content territory.'

If this interpretation is correct, the polarity flip is the network switching from 'what am I?' to 'what comes next?' — from classification to prediction. And that switch happens progressively across layers.

The polarity flip may be one of the cleanest geometric signatures of the transition from encoding to prediction in a transformer. Layer 0 MLPs classify. Layer 11 MLPs anticipate. The displacement vectors tell us when the switch happens.

Not all POS categories move independently in displacement space. Some categories have correlated displacement directions — they form alliances. Others have anti-correlated displacements — they're in opposition.
The alliances tell us which grammatical categories the MLP treats as 'moving together' — receiving similar displacement vectors and therefore playing similar roles in the transition structure.

Strongest alliances (positive displacement correlation):
• CONJ × DET: +0.360 — conjunctions and determiners move in the same direction
• CONJ × PREP: +0.342 — conjunctions and prepositions are allied
• AUX × DET: +0.305 — auxiliaries and determiners move together

Strongest oppositions (negative displacement correlation):
• DET × NOUN/VERB: -0.249 — determiners and content words move in opposite directions
• CONJ × CONTENT: -0.167 — conjunctions oppose content words

Picture a political map. On one side, you have the Function Word Coalition: determiners, prepositions, conjunctions, and auxiliaries. They vote together, move together, and reinforce each other's positions. On the other side, the Content Word Bloc: nouns, verbs, adjectives. These two groups are in perpetual opposition — when one moves right in displacement space, the other moves left.

This isn't a design decision. Nobody told GPT-2 about function words. The coalition emerged from training on text — because function words genuinely DO behave as a coalition in natural language. They co-occur, they predict each other, and they jointly scaffold the content words that fill in meaning.

The alliance between CONJ and DET (+0.360) makes linguistic sense: 'and the', 'but the', 'or a' are extremely common bigrams. The MLP has learned that conjunctions and determiners play similar structural roles — they're both 'expect a noun phrase next' signals.

The POS-level analysis treats all content words as one category. But within that category, semantic subclusters emerge in the displacement vectors. The MLP doesn't just know 'this is a content word' — it knows what KIND of content word.
We measured within-cluster cosine similarity for semantic groups of content words:

• TIME words (yesterday, always, soon): within-cluster similarity = 0.288
• MOTION/ACTION words (ran, jumped, built): 0.271
• ANIMATE words (cat, dog, girl, teacher): 0.214
• PLACE words (house, garden, street): 0.182

TIME and MOTION/ACTION words have the tightest clusters. This makes sense — temporal words have very constrained transition statistics (after 'yesterday' you almost always get a past-tense verb), and motion verbs strongly predict directional prepositions ('ran to', 'jumped over'). The MLP displacement captures these predictions.

We also found strong position effects. The word 'the' at positions 1-2 (sentence-initial) versus position 9+ has a cosine similarity of only +0.120 between their displacement vectors. Same word, same POS, but the MLP treats sentence-initial 'the' as a fundamentally different grammatical event than mid-sentence 'the'. Because it IS — sentence-initial 'the' typically introduces a subject, while later 'the' introduces objects or prepositional complements.

The position sensitivity of displacements means the MLP isn't just encoding static POS categories. It's encoding context-dependent grammatical roles. 'The' as subject-introducer and 'the' as object-introducer get pushed in different directions.

props.title: The Attention Baseline: MLP Leads, Attention Follows

children.children: A natural objection: maybe attention patterns encode the same transition structure, and the MLP is just reflecting what attention already computed. We tested this by building the same POS transition matrices from attention weights instead of MLP displacements, then correlating them.
The result is one of the strongest findings in this work:

• Layer 0: Attention ↔ MLP correlation r = -0.09 (NO relationship)
• Layer 5: r = -0.39 (actually ANTI-correlated!)
• Layer 11: r = +0.77 (p = 0.0004 — now they AGREE)

In early layers, MLP and attention encode DIFFERENT syntactic transition structures. By the final layer, they converge. The MLP isn't copying attention — it's doing independent syntactic work that attention only catches up to later. The same pattern holds for GPT-2 Medium: uncorrelated at L0, anti-correlated at L11, converged at L23 (r = +0.66).

This means the MLP is a genuinely independent source of syntactic information. It's not redundant with attention — it's complementary. Early-layer MLPs do syntactic heavy lifting that attention hasn't figured out yet.

The anti-correlation at Layer 5 (r = -0.39) is intriguing. It suggests that MLP and attention are encoding complementary information — transitions that MLP encodes strongly, attention encodes weakly, and vice versa. This is exactly what you'd expect from an efficient system: don't duplicate work. The residual stream lets MLP and attention specialize on different aspects of syntactic prediction, then combine their contributions.

Correlational evidence is nice. But does the MLP actually CAUSE correct POS prediction? We tested this by zeroing out the MLP output at each layer of GPT-2 Small and measuring the change in next-token POS accuracy.
The results are unambiguous:

• Zero Layer 0 MLP: POS accuracy DROPS 35.4 points (67.7% → 32.3%). Logit diff: -6.32.
• Zero Layer 3 MLP: No change (0.0%). Logit diff: -0.45.
• Zero Layer 5 MLP: Negligible (-0.5%). Logit diff: -0.51.
• Zero Layer 8 MLP: Negligible (-1.0%). Logit diff: -0.49.
• Zero Layer 11 MLP: Actually HELPS (+2.6%). Logit diff: -0.48.

Layer 0 MLP is CATASTROPHICALLY important for POS prediction. Kill it and accuracy halves. No other layer comes close. This is causal evidence that Layer 0 MLP is THE syntactic layer — the one that establishes grammatical scaffolding for everything downstream.

The Layer 11 result is delicious: ablating the final MLP slightly IMPROVES POS accuracy. This suggests the final layer MLP adds fine-grained token-specific information that actually interferes with coarse POS prediction. It's moved beyond grammar into the weeds of specific word choice.

Imagine inspecting a building by removing one floor at a time. Remove the foundation (Layer 0): the entire building collapses. Remove the second floor (Layer 3): nothing happens, the structure above bridges the gap. Remove the penthouse (Layer 11): the building actually gets slightly sturdier — the decorative additions were adding unnecessary weight. The grammatical load is borne almost entirely by the foundation.
Let's step back. What have we actually found?

MLPs in GPT-2 don't just transform individual token vectors. Their displacement vectors — the specific push they apply to each token — encode a syntactic transition prior. Given that I just processed a preposition, the displacement points toward the input region where determiners live. Given that I just processed a pronoun, the displacement points toward auxiliaries.
This is grammar, implemented as geometry.
The implications are significant:

1. MLPs are not just 'key-value memories.' Their displacements carry grammatical transition information — replicated across GPT-2 Small/Medium/Large and Pythia 410M, with permutation-tested significance (p<0.001).

2. The grammatical signal is front-loaded (27-71% at Layer 0, decaying with depth), and Layer 0 MLP is CAUSALLY essential — ablating it destroys POS prediction by 35 points.

3. MLP and attention encode DIFFERENT transition structures in early layers but converge by the final layer. The MLP leads; attention follows.

4. Larger models encode dramatically more sequential structure: GPT-2 Large gets 21.2% next-token accuracy from displacement geometry alone (243× chance) at ~50% network depth.

5. With proper POS tagging (NLTK Penn Treebank) on 16,000+ tokens from the Brown corpus, 39 out of 83 POS transitions are statistically significant — nearly half of all grammatical bigrams are encoded in MLP displacement geometry.

The displacement vectors are a previously unexamined channel of information in transformers. Attention heads have been studied extensively. Residual stream directions have been probed. But the specific direction an MLP pushes a token — and how that direction relates to other tokens' inputs — appears to be a rich carrier of syntactic structure. Grammar doesn't live in any single component. It lives in the geometry of MLP displacements.

If the residual stream is a river, the MLP displacements are the riverbed — the hidden topography that shapes the flow. The water (token representations) follows paths carved by the terrain (displacement geometry). And that terrain isn't random. It's been shaped by millions of sentences into channels that correspond to the transition structure of English grammar. Prepositions carve channels that lead to determiners. Pronouns carve channels that lead to auxiliaries. The grammar IS the landscape.

questions.question: Why is it necessary to subtract the mean displacement vector before analyzing MLP displacements?
options: The mean displacement dominates cosine similarities, making all tokens look similar and hiding token-specific structure
The mean displacement contains errors that need to be corrected
Subtracting the mean makes the vectors shorter and easier to compute with
The mean displacement only affects Layer 0 and can be ignored at other layers
questions.explanation: The mean displacement is a single bulk direction shared by all tokens — the 'average MLP effect.' It dominates cosine similarities, making everything look similar. Subtracting it reveals the token-specific deviations that encode grammatical structure.

questions.question: What does PC1 of the mean-subtracted displacement vectors at Layer 0 primarily separate?
options: Common words from rare words
Function words (the, a, on) from content words (cat, dog, house)
Nouns from verbs
Short words from long words
questions.explanation: PC1 captures 25.5% of variance and cleanly separates function words (determiners, prepositions, auxiliaries) from content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives). This is the dominant axis of variation in MLP displacement space.

questions.question: What happens when you zero out Layer 0 MLP in GPT-2 Small?
options: Nothing noticeable — other layers compensate
POS prediction accuracy drops by 35 points — catastrophic
The model generates random text
Only PREP→DET transitions are affected
questions.explanation: Zeroing Layer 0 MLP drops POS prediction accuracy from 67.7% to 32.3% — a 35-point collapse. No other layer ablation has more than a 1% effect. Layer 0 MLP is THE syntactic layer.

questions.question: How do attention and MLP transition structures compare across layers?
options: They encode identical structure at all layers
They are always anti-correlated — doing opposite things
Uncorrelated/anti-correlated in early layers, converging at the final layer
Attention leads and MLP copies attention's patterns
questions.explanation: At Layer 0, MLP and attention transition matrices have r ≈ -0.09 (no relationship). At Layer 5, they're anti-correlated (r = -0.39). By Layer 11, they converge (r = +0.77). The MLP does independent syntactic work early on that attention only catches up to later.

questions.question: When matching MLP outputs to inputs across tokens, 41% of matches follow which POS transition?
options: NOUN → VERB
DET → NOUN
PREP → DET
VERB → ADV
questions.explanation: 41% of endpoint matches are PREP→DET (e.g., 'on' → 'the'). This is the strongest signal in the displacement geometry and reflects the fact that preposition-determiner is one of the most common and predictable syntactic bigrams in English.
